data.notes1: overview of the MCP server module:
data.notes2: This file builds an MCP Server (SDK McpServer) in the same Node process as the client (InMemoryTransport).
data.notes3: Each registerTool() adds a tool definition—name, description, and Zod/input schema—on the server.
data.notes4: The connected MCP client discovers those definitions when it sends tools/list; the server answers with the catalog, so the server is the source of truth for which tools exist and their shapes.
data.notes5: When the client sends tools/call with a tool name and JSON arguments, the matching async handler below runs on this server side: the server both advertises and executes tools (the client only invokes; it does not run the business logic).
data.notes6: Here get_weather returns random fake conditions for teaching; get_time uses Date/toLocaleString (real clock, no external weather API).
data.notes7: In production, handlers would typically await fetch() to HTTP weather APIs, query databases, call internal microservices, queues, etc.—MCP is just the RPC boundary; tool bodies are normal async I/O.
data.notes8: The server is the source of truth for which tools exist and their shapes.

data.name: get_weather
data.notes: invoking the handler of the tool- faking the weather data
